summary: This design document outlines a secure multi-tenant container orchestrator for running untrusted third-party code in isolation. Frazelle argues for using Firecracker VMs instead of plain containers to allow full syscall access while maintaining security through multiple isolation layers. The design emphasizes defense in depth with locked-down host OSes, network deny-all defaults, eBPF monitoring, and explicit rejection of Kubernetes due to its complexity and large attack surface.
key_points: Use hypervisor-based isolation (Firecracker) instead of containers to allow full syscall interface while maintaining security
Multiple isolation layers so compromising one layer doesn't compromise the system
Network locked down by default with deny-all policy for ingress and egress
Kubernetes rejected as too complex with too large an attack surface for security-critical multi-tenancy
eBPF used extensively for monitoring, network isolation, and emergency vulnerability mitigation
Scheduler should avoid bin packing and optimize for image pull proximity
money_quotes: If one layer of isolation is compromised, rely on another layer of isolation entirely. If two layers are compromised then we at least tried our best…
I don't want the security of something that allows arbitrary code execution to have only one layer of security which someone might inadvertently turn off.
Kubernetes is hard to secure… there are a lot of components and there is no isolation between etcd, and the kubelet to apiserver communication cannot be isolated either.
Even Google doesn't use Kubernetes internally to schedule VMs, that is a whole separate thing.
If we are running on bare metal we need to account for power management, BIOS updates, hardware failures and more. These are all things the orchestration tools of today completely ignore.
By keeping our implementation more simple it is easier to keep track of the components' communication and ensure it is secure. The surface area is WAYYY smaller.
key_insight: True multi-tenant security for arbitrary code execution requires defense in depth with VM-level isolation, and Kubernetes' complexity makes it unsuitable for high-security multi-tenancy despite its popularity.
